She has called the continual reinvention "liberating", and has been drawn to it since childhood.[[336]](#cite_note-340) Gaga has said that [synesthesia](/wiki/Synesthesia "Synesthesia"), characterized by her seeing colors while making music, has played a key role in her creative process, explaining: "As I'm writing, it assembles in my brain, then through the recording it becomes a full piece of colour. Every song is a different shade."[[274]](#cite_note-Elle_2025-277) Gaga's music spans a variety of music genres, primarily [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music"),[[34]](#cite_note-Allmusic_bio-35)[[337]](#cite_note-Jordan_Page_standard-341) [dance](/wiki/Dance_music "Dance music"),[[337]](#cite_note-Jordan_Page_standard-341)[[338]](#cite_note-Xander_Zellner-342) [electronic](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music"),[[338]](#cite_note-Xander_Zellner-342) and [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz").[[339]](#cite_note-343) She has also branched out into [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"), [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco"), [Americana](/wiki/Americana_music "Americana music"), and [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music").[[337]](#cite_note-Jordan_Page_standard-341) Gaga's voice has been classified as a [contralto](/wiki/Contralto "Contralto"),[[340]](#cite_note-344)[[341]](#cite_note-345)[[342]](#cite_note-346) with a [vocal range](/wiki/Vocal_range "Vocal range") spanning from [B♭2](/wiki/B%E2%99%AD_(musical_note) "B♭ (musical note)") to [B5](/wiki/B_(musical_note) "B (musical note)").[[343]](#cite_note-Corner-347)[[344]](#cite_note-348)[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDicker2017ii-349) She has changed her vocal style regularly, and deemed *Born This Way* "much more vocally up to par with what I've always been capable of".[[346]](#cite_note-350)[[347]](#cite_note-351) In summing up her voice, *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* wrote: "There's an immense emotional intelligence behind the way she uses her voice. Almost never does she overwhelm a song with her vocal ability, recognizing instead that artistry is to be found in nuance rather than lung power."[[348]](#cite_note-352)

According to Evan Sawdey of *[PopMatters](/wiki/PopMatters "PopMatters")*, Gaga managed "to get you moving and grooving at an almost effortless pace" with *The Fame*.[[349]](#cite_note-popmatters-353) Gaga has said that she believes "all good music can be played on a piano and still sound like a hit".[[350]](#cite_note-354) [Simon Reynolds](/wiki/Simon_Reynolds "Simon Reynolds") wrote in 2010: "Everything about Gaga came from [electroclash](/wiki/Electroclash "Electroclash"), except the music, which wasn't particularly 1980s, just ruthlessly catchy [naughties](/wiki/Noughties "Noughties") pop glazed with [Auto-Tune](/wiki/Auto-Tune "Auto-Tune") and undergirded with [R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B")-ish beats."[[351]](#cite_note-355)

Gaga's songs have covered a wide variety of concepts; *The Fame* discusses the lust for stardom, while *The Fame Monster* expresses fame's dark side through [monster](/wiki/Paranoia "Paranoia") metaphors. *The Fame* is an electropop and dance-pop album that has influences of 1980s pop and 1990s [Europop](/wiki/Europop "Europop"),[[352]](#cite_note-356) whereas *The Fame Monster* displays Gaga's taste for pastiche, drawing on "Seventies arena glam, perky [ABBA](/wiki/ABBA "ABBA") [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco"), and sugary throwbacks like [Stacey Q](/wiki/Stacey_Q "Stacey Q")".[[353]](#cite_note-albumguide-357) *Born This Way* has lyrics in English, French, German, and Spanish and features themes common to Gaga's controversial songwriting such as sex, love, religion, money, drugs, identity, liberation, sexuality, freedom, and individualism.[[354]](#cite_note-btwrs-358) The album explores new genres, such as [electronic rock](/wiki/Electronic_rock "Electronic rock") and [techno](/wiki/Techno "Techno").[[355]](#cite_note-359)

The themes in *Artpop* revolve around Gaga's personal views of fame, love, sex, [feminism](/wiki/Feminism "Feminism"), self-empowerment, overcoming addiction, and reactions to media scrutiny.[[356]](#cite_note-360) *Billboard* described *Artpop* as "coherently channeling R&B, techno, disco and rock music".[[357]](#cite_note-361) With *Cheek to Cheek*, Gaga pursued the jazz genre.[[358]](#cite_note-362) *Joanne*, exploring the genres of country, funk, pop, dance, rock, electronic music and folk, was influenced by her personal life.[[359]](#cite_note-jlg-363) The *A Star Is Born* soundtrack contains elements of [blues rock](/wiki/Blues_rock "Blues rock"), country and [bubblegum pop](/wiki/Bubblegum_pop "Bubblegum pop").[[213]](#cite_note-TWP-216) *Billboard* commented that its lyrics are about wanting change, its struggle, love, romance, and bonding, describing the music as "timeless, emotional, gritty and earnest. They sound like songs written by artists who, quite frankly, are supremely messed up but hit to the core of the listener."[[360]](#cite_note-364)

On *Chromatica*, Gaga returned to her dance-pop roots, and discussed her struggles with mental health.[[361]](#cite_note-365) *Love for Sale* and *Harlequin* expand on her venture into jazz music. The former consists of a tribute to [Cole Porter](/wiki/Cole_Porter "Cole Porter"), while the latter was inspired by her role in *Joker: Folie à Deux*.[[362]](#cite_note-366)[[363]](#cite_note-367) With *Mayhem*, Gaga aimed to revisit "the pop music my earliest fans loved."[[364]](#cite_note-djmag-368) Described by *Billboard* as a "chaotic blur of genres",[[365]](#cite_note-billboard-mayhem-369) it incorporates [synth-pop](/wiki/Synth-pop "Synth-pop"),[[366]](#cite_note-slant-mayhem-370) with [industrial dance](/wiki/Industrial_dance "Industrial dance") influences,[[364]](#cite_note-djmag-368) and elements of [electro](/wiki/Electro_(music) "Electro (music)"), disco, [industrial pop](/wiki/Industrial_pop "Industrial pop"), rock and [pop rock](/wiki/Pop_rock "Pop rock").[[365]](#cite_note-billboard-mayhem-369)[[366]](#cite_note-slant-mayhem-370) Lyrically, it explores themes of love, chaos, fame, identity, and desire, using metaphors of transformation, duality, and excess.[[367]](#cite_note-371)[[368]](#cite_note-372)[[369]](#cite_note-373)

### Videos and stage

[![A pale-skinned woman holding her hands crossed and intertwined in the air. She has yellow hair and wears a low-cut bodysuit. Her chest is marked with red liquid.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Lady_Gaga_performing_Teeth_on_The_Monster_Ball_Tour%2C_London%2C_May_2010.jpg/250px-Lady_Gaga_performing_Teeth_on_The_Monster_Ball_Tour%2C_London%2C_May_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lady_Gaga_performing_Teeth_on_The_Monster_Ball_Tour,_London,_May_2010.jpg)

Gaga during a "blood-soaked" performance in 2010.

[![A woman in drag, wearing a black short wig, and a black suit paired with a white t-shirt. She is holding an award in her right hand, while her left hand is in the pocket of her trousers.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Lady_Gaga_as_Jo_Calderone_at_2011_MTV_Video_Music_Awards_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Lady_Gaga_as_Jo_Calderone_at_2011_MTV_Video_Music_Awards_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lady_Gaga_as_Jo_Calderone_at_2011_MTV_Video_Music_Awards_(cropped).jpg)

Gaga as her male [alter ego](/wiki/Alter_ego "Alter ego") Jo Calderone in 2011.

Featuring constant costume changes and provocative visuals, Gaga's music videos are often described as short films.[[370]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGray_II201296;_183-374) The video for "Telephone" earned Gaga the *Guinness World Record* for Most Product Placement in a Video.[[371]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGWR2014172-375) According to author Curtis Fogel, she explores [bondage](/wiki/Bondage_(sexual) "Bondage (sexual)") and [sadomasochism](/wiki/Sadomasochism "Sadomasochism") and highlights prevalent feminist themes. The main themes of her music videos are sex, violence, and power. She has called herself "a little bit of a feminist" and asserted that she is "sexually empowering women".[[372]](#cite_note-ultimatepredator-376) *Billboard* ranked her sixth on its list of "The 100 Greatest Music Video Artists of All Time" in 2020, stating that "the name 'Lady Gaga' will forever be synonymous with culture-shifting music videos".[[373]](#cite_note-377)

Regarded as "one of the greatest living musical performers" by *Rolling Stone*,[[374]](#cite_note-378) Gaga has called herself a perfectionist when it comes to her elaborate shows.[[375]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParvis201061-379) Her performances have been described as "highly entertaining and innovative";[[376]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllisonGoethals201331-380) the blood-spurting performance of "Paparazzi" at the [2009 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2009_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2009 MTV Video Music Awards") was described as "eye-popping" by MTV News and ranked among the best MTV VMA performances of all time by various publications.[[377]](#cite_note-381)[[378]](#cite_note-382) She continued the blood-soaked theme during The Monster Ball Tour, causing protests in England from family groups and fans in the aftermath of the [Cumbria shootings](/wiki/Cumbria_shootings "Cumbria shootings"), in which a taxi driver had killed 12 people, then himself.[[379]](#cite_note-383) At the [2011 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2011_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2011 MTV Video Music Awards"), Gaga appeared in [drag](/wiki/Drag_(clothing) "Drag (clothing)") as her male [alter ego](/wiki/Alter_ego "Alter ego"), Jo Calderone, and delivered a lovesick monologue before performing her song "You and I".[[380]](#cite_note-384) She stated that she created the character to explore "what I was looking for in men, and also what I was maybe lacking in myself".[[381]](#cite_note-thr-carson-385) When asked about the persona in 2025, she said that Calderone was "no longer with us".[[381]](#cite_note-thr-carson-385) As Gaga's choreographer and creative director, [Laurieann Gibson](/wiki/Laurieann_Gibson "Laurieann Gibson") provided material for her shows and videos for four years before she was replaced by her assistant [Richard Jackson](/wiki/Richard_Jackson_(choreographer) "Richard Jackson (choreographer)") in 2014.[[382]](#cite_note-byebyegibson-386)

In an October 2018 article for *Billboard*, Rebecca Schiller traced back Gaga's videography from "Just Dance" to the release of *A Star Is Born*. Schiller noted that following the *Artpop* era, Gaga's stripped-down approach to music was reflected in the clips for the singles from *Joanne*, taking the example of the music video of lead single "Perfect Illusion" where she eschewed "the elaborate outfits for shorts and a tee-shirt as she performed the song at a desert party". It continued with her performances in the film as well as her stage persona.[[383]](#cite_note-387) Reviewing The Chromatica Ball in 2022, Chris Willman of *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* wrote that Gaga "could have further played the authenticity card for all it's worth" after the release of *Joanne* and *A Star Is Born*, but instead "has determined to keep herself weird — or just weird enough to provide necessarily ballast to her more earnest inclinations".[[384]](#cite_note-388)

## Public image

[![A realistic mannequin of a pale-skinned woman with blonde hair wearing a hat in the design of an old-fashioned telephone.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Lady_Gaga_wax_figure_at_Madame_Tussauds_in_London.jpg/250px-Lady_Gaga_wax_figure_at_Madame_Tussauds_in_London.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lady_Gaga_wax_figure_at_Madame_Tussauds_in_London.jpg)

In 2010, eight wax figures of Gaga were installed at the museum [Madame Tussauds](/wiki/Madame_Tussauds "Madame Tussauds").[[385]](#cite_note-389)

Public reception of Gaga's music, fashion sense, and persona is polarized. She is noted for her outlandish fashion sense, which has served as an important aspect of her character.[[328]](#cite_note-ourladyvogue-332)[[331]](#cite_note-Times_Online.co.uk-335) During her early career, members of the media compared her fashion choices to those of Christina Aguilera.[[331]](#cite_note-Times_Online.co.uk-335) When she met briefly with then-president [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") at a [Human Rights Campaign](/wiki/Human_Rights_Campaign "Human Rights Campaign") fundraiser, he found the interaction "intimidating" as she was dressed in 16-inch (40 cm) heels, making her the tallest woman in the room.[[386]](#cite_note-390) When interviewed by [Barbara Walters](/wiki/Barbara_Walters "Barbara Walters") for her annual [ABC News](/wiki/ABC_News_(United_States) "ABC News (United States)") special *[10 Most Fascinating People](/wiki/Barbara_Walters%27_10_Most_Fascinating_People "Barbara Walters' 10 Most Fascinating People")* in 2009, she dismissed the claim that she is [intersex](/wiki/Intersex "Intersex") as an [urban legend](/wiki/Urban_legend "Urban legend"). Responding to a question on this issue, she expressed her fondness for [androgyny](/wiki/Androgyny "Androgyny").[[387]](#cite_note-walters-391)

In 2011, 121 women gathered at the [Grammy Awards](/wiki/53rd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "53rd Annual Grammy Awards") dressed in costumes similar to those worn by Gaga, earning the 2011 *Guinness World Record* for Largest Gathering of Lady Gaga Impersonators.[[100]](#cite_note-champ-102) The [Global Language Monitor](/wiki/Global_Language_Monitor "Global Language Monitor") named "Lady Gaga" as the Top Fashion Buzzword with her trademark "no pants" a close third.[[388]](#cite_note-nopants-392) *Entertainment Weekly* put her outfits on its end of the decade "best-of" list, saying that she "brought performance art into the mainstream".[[389]](#cite_note-393) *[People](/wiki/People_(magazine) "People (magazine)")* ranked her number one on their "Best Dressed Stars of 2021" list, writing that Gaga "strutted the streets in high-fashion designs, from a sculptural seersucker number to a black lace corseted gown—accessorizing each with elegant updos, sky-high heels and retro shades—like it was no sweat."[[390]](#cite_note-394)

*Time* placed Gaga on their All-*Time* 100 Fashion Icons list, stating: "Lady Gaga is just as notorious for her outrageous style as she is for her pop hits", mentioning outfits "made from plastic bubbles, [Kermit the Frog](/wiki/Kermit_the_Frog "Kermit the Frog") dolls, and raw meat".[[391]](#cite_note-395) Gaga wore a [dress made of raw beef](/wiki/Meat_dress_of_Lady_Gaga "Meat dress of Lady Gaga") to the 2010 MTV Video Music Awards, which was supplemented by boots, a purse, and a hat also made out of raw beef.[[392]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-396) Partly awarded in recognition of the dress, *[Vogue](/wiki/Vogue_(magazine) "Vogue (magazine)")* named her one of the Best Dressed people of 2010 and *Time* named the dress the Fashion Statement of the year.[[393]](#cite_note-397)[[394]](#cite_note-398) It attracted the attention of worldwide media; the animal rights organization [People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals](/wiki/People_for_the_Ethical_Treatment_of_Animals "People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals") (PETA) found it offensive.[[395]](#cite_note-399) The meat dress was displayed at the [National Museum of Women in the Arts](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Women_in_the_Arts "National Museum of Women in the Arts") in 2012,[[396]](#cite_note-400) and entered the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame") in September 2015.[[397]](#cite_note-401)

Gaga's fans call her "Mother Monster", and she often refers to them as "Little Monsters", a phrase she had tattooed on herself in dedication.[[398]](#cite_note-monsters-402) In his article "Lady Gaga Pioneered Online Fandom Culture As We Know It" for *[Vice](/wiki/Vice_(magazine) "Vice (magazine)")*, Jake Hall wrote that Gaga inspired several subsequent fan-brandings, such as those of [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift"), [Rihanna](/wiki/Rihanna "Rihanna") and [Justin Bieber](/wiki/Justin_Bieber "Justin Bieber").[[399]](#cite_note-403) In July 2012, Gaga also co-founded the [social networking service](/wiki/Social_networking_service "Social networking service") LittleMonsters.com, devoted to her fans.[[400]](#cite_note-404) Scott Hardy, CEO of Polaroid, praised Gaga for inspiring fans and for her close interactions with them on social media.[[401]](#cite_note-405)

### Censorship

In 2011, lyrics of Gaga's song "Born This Way", which reference homosexuality and LGBTQ subjects, were censored by Malaysian broadcaster [AMP Radio Networks](/wiki/AMP_Radio_Networks "AMP Radio Networks") as a precaution against government restrictions against songs which might violate "good taste or decency or [are] offensive to public feeling".[[402]](#cite_note-406) The same year, the [Ministry of Culture of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Ministry_of_Culture_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Ministry of Culture of the People's Republic of China"), reportedly enforcing a directive designed to tackle "poor taste and vulgar content", banned the publication of six of Gaga's songs from the album *Born This Way* on all Chinese music websites.[[403]](#cite_note-407)[[404]](#cite_note-408)

In 2014, her album *Artpop* was released in China with conditions being placed on the album artwork, covering her almost naked body as well as changing the title of the song "[Sexxx Dreams](/wiki/Sexxx_Dreams "Sexxx Dreams")" to "X Dreams".[[405]](#cite_note-409) In 2016, her meeting with the [Dalai Lama](/wiki/14th_Dalai_Lama "14th Dalai Lama") resulted in the [Chinese government](/wiki/Chinese_government "Chinese government") adding her to a list of hostile foreign forces, and Chinese websites and media organizations were ordered to stop distributing her songs.[[406]](#cite_note-410)[[407]](#cite_note-411) The [Publicity Department of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Publicity_Department_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Publicity Department of the Chinese Communist Party") also issued an order for state-controlled media to condemn the meeting.[[408]](#cite_note-412) In 2019, coverage of Gaga in the 91st Academy Awards was removed on some Chinese streaming platforms such as [Mango TV](/wiki/Mango_TV "Mango TV") and in 2021 her appearance was cut from *Friends: The Reunion*; both incidents received backlash from Chinese fans.[[409]](#cite_note-413)[[410]](#cite_note-414)

## Activism

### Philanthropy

After declining an invitation to appear on the single "[We Are the World 25 for Haiti](/wiki/We_Are_the_World_25_for_Haiti "We Are the World 25 for Haiti")", because of rehearsals for her tour, to benefit victims of the [2010 Haiti earthquake](/wiki/2010_Haiti_earthquake "2010 Haiti earthquake"), Gaga donated the proceeds of her January 2010 [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall") concert to the country's reconstruction relief fund.[[411]](#cite_note-415) All profits from her online store that day were also donated, and Gaga announced that $500,000 was collected for the fund.[[412]](#cite_note-416) Hours after the [2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami](/wiki/2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami "2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami") hit Japan, Gaga tweeted a link to Japan Prayer Bracelets. All revenue from a bracelet she designed in conjunction with the company was donated to relief efforts;[[413]](#cite_note-417) these raised $1.5 million.[[414]](#cite_note-418) In June 2011, Gaga performed at [MTV Japan](/wiki/MTV_Japan "MTV Japan")'s charity show in [Makuhari Messe](/wiki/Makuhari_Messe "Makuhari Messe"), which benefited the [Japanese Red Cross](/wiki/Japanese_Red_Cross "Japanese Red Cross").[[415]](#cite_note-419)

In 2012, Gaga joined the campaign group [Artists Against Fracking](/wiki/Artists_Against_Fracking "Artists Against Fracking").[[416]](#cite_note-420) That October, [Yoko Ono](/wiki/Yoko_Ono "Yoko Ono") gave Gaga and four other activists the [LennonOno Grant for Peace](/wiki/LennonOno_Grant_for_Peace "LennonOno Grant for Peace") in [Reykjavík](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk "Reykjavík"), Iceland.[[417]](#cite_note-421) The following month, Gaga pledged to donate $1 million to the American Red Cross to help the victims of [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy"). Gaga has also contributed to the fight against [HIV and AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS"), focusing on educating young women about the risks of the disease. In collaboration with [Cyndi Lauper](/wiki/Cyndi_Lauper "Cyndi Lauper"), Gaga joined forces with [MAC Cosmetics](/wiki/Make-up_Art_Cosmetics "Make-up Art Cosmetics") to launch a line of lipstick under their supplementary cosmetic line, Viva Glam.[[418]](#cite_note-422) Sales have raised more than $202 million to fight HIV and AIDS.[[419]](#cite_note-423)

In April 2016, Gaga joined Vice President Joe Biden at the [University of Nevada, Las Vegas](/wiki/University_of_Nevada,_Las_Vegas "University of Nevada, Las Vegas") to support his [It's On Us](/wiki/White_House_Task_Force_to_Protect_Students_from_Sexual_Assault "White House Task Force to Protect Students from Sexual Assault") campaign as he traveled to colleges on behalf of the organization, which has seen 250,000 students from more than 530 colleges sign a pledge of solidarity and activism.[[420]](#cite_note-424) Two months later, Gaga attended the [84th Annual US Conference of Mayors](/wiki/United_States_Conference_of_Mayors "United States Conference of Mayors") in Indianapolis where together with the Dalai Lama she talked about the power of kindness and how to make the world a more compassionate place.[[421]](#cite_note-425)

In April 2020, Gaga curated the televised benefit concert, *[One World: Together at Home](/wiki/Together_at_Home "Together at Home")*, a collaboration with [Global Citizen](/wiki/Global_Citizen_(organization) "Global Citizen (organization)") to benefit the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization")'s [COVID-19 Solidarity Response Fund](/wiki/COVID-19_Solidarity_Response_Fund "COVID-19 Solidarity Response Fund").[[422]](#cite_note-WHO-426)[[423]](#cite_note-427) The special raised $127 million, which according to *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* "puts it on par with the other legendary fundraiser, [Live Aid](/wiki/Live_Aid "Live Aid"), as the highest grossing charity concert in history."[[424]](#cite_note-428) In recognition of her activism for the [Black Lives Matter](/wiki/Black_Lives_Matter "Black Lives Matter") movement during the 2020 [George Floyd protests](/wiki/George_Floyd_protests "George Floyd protests"), Gaga received the [Yolanda Denise King](/wiki/Yolanda_Denise_King "Yolanda Denise King") High Ground Award from the [King Center](/wiki/King_Center_for_Nonviolent_Social_Change "King Center for Nonviolent Social Change")'s Beloved Community Awards in January 2021. In her acceptance speech, she denounced racism and white supremacy and addressed her social responsibility as a high-profile artist and white woman.[[425]](#cite_note-429)

### Born This Way Foundation

Main article: [Born This Way Foundation](/wiki/Born_This_Way_Foundation "Born This Way Foundation")

[![Refer to caption.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/LadyGagaBornBraveBus.jpg/250px-LadyGagaBornBraveBus.jpg)](/wiki/File:LadyGagaBornBraveBus.jpg)

Gaga during an event for the [Born This Way Foundation](/wiki/Born_This_Way_Foundation "Born This Way Foundation") in Europe, 2013

In 2012, Gaga launched the [Born This Way Foundation](/wiki/Born_This_Way_Foundation "Born This Way Foundation") (BTWF), a non-profit organization that focuses on youth empowerment. It has been named after her 2011 single and album. Media proprietor [Oprah Winfrey](/wiki/Oprah_Winfrey "Oprah Winfrey"), writer Deepak Chopra, and US Secretary of Health and Human Services [Kathleen Sebelius](/wiki/Kathleen_Sebelius "Kathleen Sebelius") spoke at the foundation's inauguration at [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University").[[426]](#cite_note-430) The foundation's original funding included $1.2 million from Gaga, $500,000 from the MacArthur Foundation, and $850,000 from [Barneys New York](/wiki/Barneys_New_York "Barneys New York").[[427]](#cite_note-431) In July 2012, the BTWF partnered with [Office Depot](/wiki/Office_Depot "Office Depot") for a series of limited edition back-to-school products, with 25% of its sales going towards the foundation, for a guaranteed donation of $1 million.[[428]](#cite_note-432) The foundation's initiatives have included the "Born Brave Bus" that followed Gaga on tour as a youth drop-in center as an initiative against bullying.[[429]](#cite_note-433)[[430]](#cite_note-434)

In October 2015, at the Yale Center for Emotional Intelligence, Gaga joined 200 high school students, policy makers, and academic officials, including [Peter Salovey](/wiki/Peter_Salovey "Peter Salovey"), to discuss ways to recognize and channel emotions for positive outcomes.[[431]](#cite_note-435) In 2016, the foundation partnered with Intel, [Vox Media](/wiki/Vox_Media "Vox Media"), and *[Recode](/wiki/Recode "Recode")* to fight online harassment.[[432]](#cite_note-436) The sales revenue of the 99th issue of the *V* magazine, which featured Gaga and Kinney, was donated to the foundation.[[168]](#cite_note-V_mag-171) Gaga and Elton John released the clothing and accessories line Love Bravery at [Macy's](/wiki/Macy%27s "Macy's") in May 2016. 25% of each purchase benefited the BTWF and the [Elton John AIDS Foundation](/wiki/Elton_John_AIDS_Foundation "Elton John AIDS Foundation").[[433]](#cite_note-lovebravery-437) Gaga partnered with [Starbucks](/wiki/Starbucks "Starbucks") for a week in June 2017 with the "Cups of Kindness" campaign, where the company donated 25 cents from some of the beverages sold to the foundation.[[434]](#cite_note-438) She also appeared in a video by [Staples Inc.](/wiki/Staples_Inc. "Staples Inc.") to raise funds for the foundation and DonorsChoose.org.[[435]](#cite_note-439)

On the 2018 [World Kindness Day](/wiki/World_Kindness_Day "World Kindness Day"), Gaga partnered with the foundation to bring food and relief to a [Red Cross](/wiki/Red_Cross "Red Cross") shelter for people who have been forced to evacuate homes due to the [California wildfires](/wiki/2018_California_wildfires "2018 California wildfires"). The foundation also partnered with Starbucks and [SoulCycle](/wiki/SoulCycle "SoulCycle") to thank California firefighters for their relief work during the crisis. Gaga had to previously evacuate her own home during the [Woolsey Fire](/wiki/Woolsey_Fire "Woolsey Fire") which spread through parts of Malibu.[[436]](#cite_note-440) In March 2019, she penned a letter to supporters of the BTWF, announcing the launch of a new pilot program for a teen mental health first aid project with the National Council for Behavioral Health. Gaga revealed her personal struggles with [mental health](/wiki/Mental_health "Mental health") in her letter and how she was able to get support which saved her life: "I know what it means to have someone support me and understand what I'm going through, and every young person in the world should have someone to turn to when they're hurting. It saved my life, and it will save theirs."[[437]](#cite_note-441)[[438]](#cite_note-442)

In September 2020, Gaga released an anthology book, *Channel Kindness: Stories of Kindness and Community*, featuring fifty-one stories about kindness, bravery, and resilience from young people all over the world collected by the BTWF, and introduced by herself.[[439]](#cite_note-443) She promoted it with a 21 days of kindness challenge on her social media, using the "BeKind21" hashtag.[[440]](#cite_note-444) In 2021, Gaga collaborated with the Champagne house Dom Pérignon to benefit the BTWF by releasing a limited edition of Rosé Vintage 2005 bottles along with a sculpture designed by her. The 110 exclusive pieces were sold at private sales, and the profit of $570,000 was donated to the foundation.[[441]](#cite_note-445)[[442]](#cite_note-446) On the 2021 World Kindness Day, Gaga released a 30-minute special, titled *[The Power of Kindness](/wiki/The_Power_of_Kindness "The Power of Kindness")*, as part of the foundation's Channel Kindness program, in which together with a mental health expert and a group of eleven young people, she explored the connection between kindness and mental health.[[443]](#cite_note-447)

### LGBTQ advocacy

[![A woman with blonde hair speaking at a podium into several microphones. She wears large glasses. The background is a series of red and white horizontal stripes.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Gagaspeechdontask.jpg/250px-Gagaspeechdontask.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gagaspeechdontask.jpg)

Gaga speaking against "[don't ask, don't tell](/wiki/Don%27t_ask,_don%27t_tell "Don't ask, don't tell")" in [Portland, Maine](/wiki/Portland,_Maine "Portland, Maine") (2010)

A [bisexual](/wiki/Bisexuality "Bisexuality") woman,[[e]](#cite_note-448) Gaga has been actively supporting LGBTQ rights worldwide.[[444]](#cite_note-activist-449) She has attributed much of her early success as a mainstream artist to her [gay](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality") fans and is considered a gay icon.[[445]](#cite_note-fabmag-450)[[446]](#cite_note-451) Early in her career, Gaga had difficulty getting [radio airplay](/wiki/Airplay "Airplay"), and stated: "The turning point for me was the gay community."[[447]](#cite_note-mtvgayicon-452) She thanked FlyLife, a [Manhattan](/wiki/LGBTQ_culture_in_New_York_City "LGBTQ culture in New York City")-based [LGBTQ](/wiki/LGBTQ "LGBTQ") marketing company which worked with her label Interscope at the time, in the liner notes of *The Fame*.[[448]](#cite_note-thefame-453) One of her first televised performances was in May 2008 at the [NewNowNext Awards](/wiki/NewNowNext_Awards "NewNowNext Awards"), an awards show aired by the LGBTQ television network [Logo](/wiki/Logo_TV "Logo TV").[[449]](#cite_note-newnownext-454)

Gaga spoke at the 2009 [National Equality March](/wiki/National_Equality_March "National Equality March") in Washington, D.C., to support the LGBTQ rights movement.[[450]](#cite_note-455) She attended the 2010 MTV Video Music Awards accompanied by four gay and lesbian former members of the [United States Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces") who had been unable to serve [openly](/wiki/Coming_out "Coming out") under the US military's "[don't ask, don't tell](/wiki/Don%27t_ask,_don%27t_tell "Don't ask, don't tell")" policy, which banned open homosexuality in the military.[[451]](#cite_note-456) Gaga urged her fans via YouTube to contact their senators in an effort to overturn the policy. In September 2010, she spoke at a [Servicemembers Legal Defense Network](/wiki/Servicemembers_Legal_Defense_Network "Servicemembers Legal Defense Network")'s rally in [Portland, Maine](/wiki/Portland,_Maine "Portland, Maine"). Following this event, *[The Advocate](/wiki/The_Advocate_(LGBTQ_magazine) "The Advocate (LGBTQ magazine)")* named her a "fierce advocate" for gays and lesbians.[[452]](#cite_note-457)

Gaga appeared at [Europride](/wiki/Europride "Europride"), an international event dedicated to LGBTQ pride, in Rome in June 2011. She criticized the poor state of [gay rights in many European countries](/wiki/LGBTQ_rights_in_Europe "LGBTQ rights in Europe") and described gay people as "revolutionaries of love".[[453]](#cite_note-mtvrome2-458) Later that year, she was referenced by teenager [Jamey Rodemeyer](/wiki/Suicide_of_Jamey_Rodemeyer "Suicide of Jamey Rodemeyer") in the hours prior to his death, with Rodemeyer having tweeted "@ladygaga bye mother monster, thank you for all you have done, paws up forever". Rodemeyer's suicide prompted Gaga to meet with then-President Barack Obama to address anti-gay bullying in American schools.[[454]](#cite_note-guardian2-459) She later [endorsed](/wiki/List_of_Barack_Obama_2012_presidential_campaign_endorsements "List of Barack Obama 2012 presidential campaign endorsements") him during the [2012 US presidential election](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election "2012 United States presidential election").[[455]](#cite_note-460) In 2011, Gaga was also ordained as a minister by the [Universal Life Church Monastery](/wiki/Universal_Life_Church_Monastery "Universal Life Church Monastery") allowing her to officiate the wedding of two female friends.[[456]](#cite_note-461)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Lady_Gaga_-_Paparazzi_at_The_Mayhem_Ball_in_London.jpg/250px-Lady_Gaga_-_Paparazzi_at_The_Mayhem_Ball_in_London.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lady_Gaga_-_Paparazzi_at_The_Mayhem_Ball_in_London.jpg)

Gaga at her [Mayhem Ball](/wiki/The_Mayhem_Ball "The Mayhem Ball") tour in 2025, with [rainbow](/wiki/Rainbow_flag_(LGBTQ) "Rainbow flag (LGBTQ)") lights projected onto her long white cape[[457]](#cite_note-462)

In June 2016, during a vigil held in Los Angeles for victims of the [attack at the gay nightclub Pulse](/wiki/Pulse_nightclub_shooting "Pulse nightclub shooting") in Orlando, Gaga read aloud the names of the 49 people killed in the attack, and gave a speech.[[458]](#cite_note-463) She also appeared in [Human Rights Campaign](/wiki/Human_Rights_Campaign "Human Rights Campaign")'s tribute video to the victims of the attack.[[459]](#cite_note-464) Following [Joan Jett](/wiki/Joan_Jett "Joan Jett"), Gaga was the second of nearly 200 musicians and music industry executives to sign [*Billboard*'s Open Letter on Gun Violence](/wiki/Billboard%27s_Open_Letter_on_Gun_Violence "Billboard's Open Letter on Gun Violence"), demanding that Congress enact [universal background checks](/wiki/Universal_background_check "Universal background check") on all gun buyers.[[460]](#cite_note-USAToday-465) She subsequently supported the 2018 [March for Our Lives](/wiki/March_for_Our_Lives "March for Our Lives") gun-control rally in Washington, D.C.[[461]](#cite_note-466) Gaga opposed the [presidency of Donald Trump](/wiki/First_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "First presidency of Donald Trump") and his [military transgender ban](/wiki/Transgender_military_ban_in_the_United_States "Transgender military ban in the United States").[[462]](#cite_note-467)[[463]](#cite_note-468) She [supported](/wiki/List_of_Hillary_Clinton_2016_presidential_campaign_celebrity_endorsements "List of Hillary Clinton 2016 presidential campaign celebrity endorsements") former Secretary of State [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") for president in [2016](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election").[[464]](#cite_note-469) In 2018, a leaked memo from Trump's office revealed that his administration wanted to change the legal definition of sex to exclude [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") Americans. Gaga was one of the many celebrities to condemn him and spread the #WontBeErased campaign to her Twitter followers.[[465]](#cite_note-470)[[466]](#cite_note-471)

In January 2019, during one of her Enigma shows, she criticized Vice President [Mike Pence](/wiki/Mike_Pence "Mike Pence") for his wife [Karen Pence](/wiki/Karen_Pence "Karen Pence") working at an evangelical [Christian school](/wiki/Christian_school "Christian school") where LGBTQ people are turned away, calling him "the worst representation of what it means to be a Christian". Gaga also stated: "I am a Christian woman, and what I do know about Christianity is that we bear no prejudice, and everybody is welcome".[[467]](#cite_note-472) Gaga made a congratulatory speech commemorating the 50th anniversary of the [Stonewall riots](/wiki/Stonewall_riots "Stonewall riots") and the [LGBTQ community](/wiki/LGBTQ_community "LGBTQ community")'s accomplishments at [WorldPride NYC 2019](/wiki/Stonewall_50_%E2%80%93_WorldPride_NYC_2019 "Stonewall 50 – WorldPride NYC 2019") outside the [Stonewall Inn](/wiki/Stonewall_Inn "Stonewall Inn"), birthplace of the modern [gay rights movement](/wiki/LGBTQ_social_movements "LGBTQ social movements").[[468]](#cite_note-GagaStonewallNYCPride-473) Following Trump's win in the [2024 US presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election"), in which Gaga endorsed [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris"), she voiced her support for the transgender community after his [rollback of their protections](/wiki/Executive_Order_14168 "Executive Order 14168").[[469]](#cite_note-474)[[470]](#cite_note-475)

## Legacy

Gaga was named the "[Queen of Pop](/wiki/Honorific_nicknames_in_popular_music#G "Honorific nicknames in popular music")" in a 2011 ranking by *Rolling Stone* based on record sales and social media metrics.[[471]](#cite_note-Molanphy-476) In 2012, she ranked fourth in [VH1](/wiki/VH1 "VH1")'s [Greatest Women in Music](/wiki/The_Greatest_(VH1_TV_series) "The Greatest (VH1 TV series)"),[[472]](#cite_note-477) and became a feature of the temporary exhibition *The Elevated. From the Pharaoh to Lady Gaga*, which marked the 150th anniversary of the [National Museum in Warsaw](/wiki/National_Museum,_Warsaw "National Museum, Warsaw").[[473]](#cite_note-warsawvoice-478)

[![Gaga kneeling down wearing a shiny black upper garment, fishnet stockings and black high-heeled boots. Her hair is pale yellow.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Lady_Gaga%2C_ARTPOP_Ball_Tour%2C_Bell_Center%2C_Montr%C3%A9al%2C_2_July_2014_%2860%29_%2814376655959%29.jpg/250px-Lady_Gaga%2C_ARTPOP_Ball_Tour%2C_Bell_Center%2C_Montr%C3%A9al%2C_2_July_2014_%2860%29_%2814376655959%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lady_Gaga,_ARTPOP_Ball_Tour,_Bell_Center,_Montr%C3%A9al,_2_July_2014_(60)_(14376655959).jpg)

Gaga performing on the [ArtRave: The Artpop Ball](/wiki/ArtRave:_The_Artpop_Ball "ArtRave: The Artpop Ball") tour in 2014

Gaga has often been praised for using controversy to bring attention to various issues.[[474]](#cite_note-479)[[475]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarsico201277–78-480) According to Frankie Graddon of *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")*, Gaga—who wore a meat dress to highlight her distaste for the US military's "don't ask, don't tell" policy—influenced protest dressing on red carpet.[[476]](#cite_note-481) *Billboard* named her the "[Greatest Pop Star](/wiki/Billboard%27s_Greatest_Pop_Stars#By_year "Billboard's Greatest Pop Stars")" of 2009, asserting that "to say that her one-year rise from rookie to MVP was meteoric doesn't quite cut it, as she wasn't just successful, but game-changing—thanks to her voracious appetite for reinvention."[[477]](#cite_note-482) Because of *The Fame*'s success—it was listed as one of the 100 Greatest Debut Albums of All-Time by *Rolling Stone* in 2013[[478]](#cite_note-483)—Gaga has been credited as one of the musicians that popularized synth-pop in the late 2000s and early 2010s.[[479]](#cite_note-484)

According to [Kelefa Sanneh](/wiki/Kelefa_Sanneh "Kelefa Sanneh") of *[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")*, "Lady Gaga blazed a trail for truculent pop stars by treating her own celebrity as an evolving art project."[[480]](#cite_note-485) Including *Born This Way* as one of the 50 best female albums of all time, *Rolling Stone*'s [Rob Sheffield](/wiki/Rob_Sheffield "Rob Sheffield") considers it "hard to remember a world where we didn't have Gaga, although we're pretty sure it was a lot more boring".[[481]](#cite_note-486) In 2015, *Time* noted that Gaga had "practically invented the current era of pop music as spectacle".[[482]](#cite_note-487) A decade later, Daniel D'Addario of *Variety* further observed the influence of Gaga's sense of spectacle on the next generation of artists, calling her "a pop star's pop star". He surmised that [Sabrina Carpenter](/wiki/Sabrina_Carpenter "Sabrina Carpenter")'s use of self-parodying and ultra-glam [camp](/wiki/Camp_(style) "Camp (style)") in her performances, [Billie Eilish](/wiki/Billie_Eilish "Billie Eilish")'s incorporation of grandeur into her shows, and [Chappell Roan](/wiki/Chappell_Roan "Chappell Roan")'s "drag-inflected let's-put-on-a-show spirit" and "eagerness to use costumes and makeup to help tell her stories" all came from each watching the video for "Bad Romance" in their childhoods.[[483]](#cite_note-488)

A 2017 journal published by *[Psychology of Aesthetics, Creativity, and the Arts](/wiki/Psychology_of_Aesthetics,_Creativity,_and_the_Arts "Psychology of Aesthetics, Creativity, and the Arts")* studying structural patterns in melodies of [earworm](/wiki/Earworm "Earworm") songs compiled lists of catchiest tracks from 3,000 participants, in which Gaga's "Bad Romance", "Alejandro", and "Poker Face" ranked number one, eight, and nine, respectively.[[484]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJakubowskiFinkelStewartMüllensiefen2017122–135-489) In 2018, [NPR](/wiki/NPR "NPR") wrote she was the second most influential female artist of the 21st century, calling her "one of the first big artists of the 'Internet age'".[[485]](#cite_note-490) *Billboard* also placed Gaga fifth on its 2024 "[Greatest Pop Stars of the 21st Century](/wiki/Billboard%27s_Greatest_Pop_Stars#21st_century "Billboard's Greatest Pop Stars")" list,[[486]](#cite_note-491) and sixth on the 2025 "Top 100 Women Artists of the 21st Century" list.[[487]](#cite_note-492) She and her work have influenced various artists including [Miley Cyrus](/wiki/Miley_Cyrus "Miley Cyrus"),[[488]](#cite_note-493) [Nicki Minaj](/wiki/Nicki_Minaj "Nicki Minaj"),[[489]](#cite_note-494) [Ellie Goulding](/wiki/Ellie_Goulding "Ellie Goulding"),[[490]](#cite_note-495) [Halsey](/wiki/Halsey_(singer) "Halsey (singer)"),[[491]](#cite_note-496) [Jennifer Lopez](/wiki/Jennifer_Lopez "Jennifer Lopez"),[[492]](#cite_note-497) Beyoncé,[[493]](#cite_note-498) [Nick Jonas](/wiki/Nick_Jonas "Nick Jonas"),[[494]](#cite_note-499) [Sam Smith](/wiki/Sam_Smith_(singer) "Sam Smith (singer)"),[[495]](#cite_note-500) [Noah Cyrus](/wiki/Noah_Cyrus "Noah Cyrus"),[[496]](#cite_note-501) [Katherine Langford](/wiki/Katherine_Langford "Katherine Langford"),[[497]](#cite_note-502) [MGMT](/wiki/MGMT "MGMT"),[[498]](#cite_note-503) [Allie X](/wiki/Allie_X "Allie X"),[[499]](#cite_note-504) [Greyson Chance](/wiki/Greyson_Chance "Greyson Chance"),[[500]](#cite_note-505) [Cardi B](/wiki/Cardi_B "Cardi B"),[[501]](#cite_note-506) [Rina Sawayama](/wiki/Rina_Sawayama "Rina Sawayama"),[[502]](#cite_note-507) [Blackpink](/wiki/Blackpink "Blackpink"),[[503]](#cite_note-508) [Madison Beer](/wiki/Madison_Beer "Madison Beer"),[[504]](#cite_note-509) [Ren](/wiki/Ren_(South_Korean_singer) "Ren (South Korean singer)") of [NU'EST](/wiki/NU%27EST "NU'EST"),[[505]](#cite_note-510) [Slayyyter](/wiki/Slayyyter "Slayyyter"),[[506]](#cite_note-511) [Bebe Rexha](/wiki/Bebe_Rexha "Bebe Rexha"),[[507]](#cite_note-512) [Bree Runway](/wiki/Bree_Runway "Bree Runway"),[[508]](#cite_note-513) [Celeste](/wiki/Celeste_(singer) "Celeste (singer)"),[[509]](#cite_note-514) [Kim [SZA](/wiki/SZA "SZA"),[[518]](#cite_note-523) [Raye](/wiki/Raye "Raye"),[[519]](#cite_note-524) [Rebecca Black](/wiki/Rebecca_Black "Rebecca Black"),[[520]](#cite_note-525) [Grace Gaustad](/wiki/Grace_Gaustad "Grace Gaustad"),[[521]](#cite_note-526) [Laufey](/wiki/Laufey_(singer) "Laufey (singer)"),[[522]](#cite_note-527) and Chappell Roan.[[523]](#cite_note-528)

Various higher education institutions have offered courses focusing on Gaga. Due to her influence on 21st century culture and her rise to global fame, sociologist Mathieu Deflem of the [University of South Carolina](/wiki/University_of_South_Carolina "University of South Carolina") launched a course titled "Lady Gaga and the Sociology of the Fame" in early 2011 with the objective of unraveling "some of the sociologically relevant dimensions of the fame of Lady Gaga".[[524]](#cite_note-course-529) Focusing on her influence on gender and sexual identity, the [University of Virginia College of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_College_of_Arts_and_Sciences "University of Virginia College of Arts and Sciences") introduced a writing course titled "GaGa for Gaga: Sex, Gender, and Identity" during its Fall 2010 semester, which explored "how identity is challenged by gender and sexuality and how Lady Gaga confronts this challenge".[[525]](#cite_note-530) Courses exploring different aspects of Gaga's phenomenon, including her music and fame, have also been offered by [Arizona State University](/wiki/Arizona_State_University "Arizona State University"), [Tufts University](/wiki/Tufts_University "Tufts University"), [St. Catherine University](/wiki/St._Catherine_University "St. Catherine University"), and the [University of Richmond](/wiki/University_of_Richmond "University of Richmond").[[526]](#cite_note-531)

A new genus of [ferns](/wiki/Fern "Fern"), *[Gaga](/wiki/Gaga_(plant) "Gaga (plant)")*, and its species, *G. germanotta* and *G. monstraparva*, have been named in her honor. The name *monstraparva* alluded to Gaga's fans, known as Little Monsters, since their symbol is the outstretched "monster claw" hand, which resembles a tightly rolled young fern leaf prior to unfurling.[[527]](#cite_note-532) Gaga also has an extinct mammal, *[Gagadon](/wiki/Gagadon "Gagadon") minimonstrum*,[[528]](#cite_note-Stuck&Covert2014-533) a parasitic wasp, *[Aleiodes gaga](/wiki/Aleiodes_gaga "Aleiodes gaga")*,[[529]](#cite_note-534)[[530]](#cite_note-Butcheretal2012-535) and a treehopper, *[Kaikaia gaga](/wiki/Kaikaia_gaga "Kaikaia gaga")*, named for her.[[531]](#cite_note-536)

In [Taichung](/wiki/Taichung "Taichung"), Taiwan, July 3 is designated as "Lady Gaga Day" marking the first day Gaga visited the country in 2011.[[532]](#cite_note-537) In May 2021, to celebrate the tenth anniversary of *Born This Way* and its cultural impact, [West Hollywood](/wiki/West_Hollywood,_California "West Hollywood, California") mayor, [Lindsey P. Horvath](/wiki/Lindsey_P._Horvath "Lindsey P. Horvath"), presented Gaga with a [key to the city](/wiki/Key_to_the_city "Key to the city") and declared May 23 as "Born This Way Day". A street painting with the [Daniel Quasar](/wiki/Daniel_Quasar "Daniel Quasar")'s version of the [pride flag](/wiki/Rainbow_flag_(LGBTQ) "Rainbow flag (LGBTQ)") featuring the album's title was also unveiled on [Robertson Boulevard](/wiki/Robertson_Boulevard_(Los_Angeles) "Robertson Boulevard (Los Angeles)") as a tribute to the album, and how it has inspired the LGBTQ community over the years.[[533]](#cite_note-538)

## Achievements

See also: [List of awards and nominations received by Lady Gaga](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Lady_Gaga "List of awards and nominations received by Lady Gaga")

Gaga has won sixteen [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards"),[[534]](#cite_note-539) an Academy Award,[[208]](#cite_note-a_star_is_born_awards_and_nominations-211) a [Sports Emmy Award](/wiki/Sports_Emmy_Award "Sports Emmy Award"),[[300]](#cite_note-sports_emmy-303) two [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards"),[[535]](#cite_note-540) a [BAFTA Award](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Awards "British Academy Film Awards"),[[208]](#cite_note-a_star_is_born_awards_and_nominations-211) twenty two [MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/MTV_Video_Music_Awards "MTV Video Music Awards") (including the inaugural Tricon Award),[[236]](#cite_note-mtv_vma-239) three [Brit Awards](/wiki/Brit_Awards "Brit Awards"),[[536]](#cite_note-541) and eighteen *Guinness World Records*.[[537]](#cite_note-542) She has been honored with the inaugural [Songwriters Hall of Fame](/wiki/Songwriters_Hall_of_Fame "Songwriters Hall of Fame") Contemporary Icon Award as a songwriter-artist who has attained an iconic status in pop culture.[[538]](#cite_note-543) She also received the [National Arts Awards](/wiki/National_Arts_Awards "National Arts Awards")' Young Artist Award, honoring individuals who have shown accomplishments and leadership early in their career,[[539]](#cite_note-544) and the [Grammy Museum](/wiki/Grammy_Museum "Grammy Museum") Jane Ortner Artist Award, which recognizes artists who have demonstrated passion and dedication to education through the arts.[[540]](#cite_note-545) The [Council of Fashion Designers of America](/wiki/Council_of_Fashion_Designers_of_America "Council of Fashion Designers of America") recognized her with the Fashion Icon award.[[541]](#cite_note-546) In 2019, Gaga became the first person to win an Academy, BAFTA, Golden Globe, and Grammy Award in one year for her contributions to the *A Star Is Born* soundtrack.[[542]](#cite_note-r717-547) In 2023, *Rolling Stone* included her among the [200 Greatest Singers of All Time](/wiki/Rolling_Stone%27s_200_Greatest_Singers_of_All_Time "Rolling Stone's 200 Greatest Singers of All Time").[[543]](#cite_note-548)

With estimated sales of 124 million records, Gaga is one of [the world's best-selling music artists](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_music_artists "List of best-selling music artists").[[544]](#cite_note-549)[[545]](#cite_note-550) She is the only female artist to have four singles ("Just Dance", "Poker Face", "Bad Romance" and "Shallow") each sell at least 10 million copies globally.[[546]](#cite_note-GWR20-551) As of 2025, she has grossed more than $879.6 million in revenue from concert tours and residencies, with attendance of 7.1 million,[[547]](#cite_note-TourTotals2025-552) being the fifth woman to pass the half-billion total as reported to *Billboard* Boxscore,[[238]](#cite_note-chromaticaballboxscore-241)[[548]](#cite_note-bbgrossenogma-553) receiving the [*Pollstar* Award](/wiki/Pollstar "Pollstar") for Pop Touring Artist of the Decade (2010s).[[549]](#cite_note-554) She is also among the [top digital singles artists](/wiki/List_of_highest-certified_music_artists_in_the_United_States "List of highest-certified music artists in the United States") in the US, with 87.5 million equivalent units certified according to [Recording Industry Association of America](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America") (RIAA),[[f]](#cite_note-556) was the first woman to receive the Digital Diamond Award certification from RIAA,[[551]](#cite_note-557) one of the [few artists](/wiki/RIAA_certification#Most_Diamond_2 "RIAA certification") with at least three Diamond certified songs ("Bad Romance", "Poker Face" and "Just Dance"),[[552]](#cite_note-558) and the first artist to have two songs pass seven million downloads ("Poker Face" and "Just Dance").[[553]](#cite_note-559)

Named *Billboard*'s [Artist of the Year](/wiki/Billboard_Year-End_Top_Artist "Billboard Year-End Top Artist") in 2010, Gaga subsequently appeared on its "Greatest of All Time Artists" chart.[[554]](#cite_note-560)[[555]](#cite_note-561) She became the longest-reigning act of *Billboard*'s [Dance/Electronic Albums](/wiki/Dance/Electronic_Albums "Dance/Electronic Albums") chart with 211 weeks at number one, while *The Fame* spent a record 142 weeks atop the chart.[[556]](#cite_note-562) As of 2020, six of her singles appeared on the [International Federation of the Phonographic Industry](/wiki/International_Federation_of_the_Phonographic_Industry "International Federation of the Phonographic Industry")'s annual Top 10 Global Singles chart ("Just Dance", "Poker Face", "Bad Romance", "Telephone", "Born This Way" and "Shallow"), setting the record for the most entries by any female artist.[[546]](#cite_note-GWR20-551)

According to *Guinness World Records*, Gaga was the most followed person on Twitter from 2011 to 2013,[[100]](#cite_note-champ-102)[[557]](#cite_note-563) the most famous celebrity in 2013,[[558]](#cite_note-564) and the most powerful popstar in 2014.[[559]](#cite_note-565) She was included on *Forbes*' [Celebrity 100](/wiki/Forbes_Celebrity_100 "Forbes Celebrity 100") from 2010 to 2015 and 2018 to 2020, topping the list in 2011.[[560]](#cite_note-566) Gaga also appeared on their list of the [World's Most Powerful Women](/wiki/Forbes_list_of_The_World%27s_100_Most_Powerful_Women "Forbes list of The World's 100 Most Powerful Women") from 2010 to 2014, and topped their list of the Most Powerful Musicians in 2013.[[561]](#cite_note-567)[[562]](#cite_note-568) She was named one of the [100 most influential people in the world](/wiki/Time_100 "Time 100") by *Time* magazine in 2010 and 2019,[[563]](#cite_note-569)[[564]](#cite_note-570) and ranked second in its most influential people of the past ten years readers' poll in 2013.[[565]](#cite_note-Fusetv-571)

As of October 2020, *Forbes* estimated Gaga's [net worth](/wiki/Net_worth "Net worth") at $150 million.[[g]](#cite_note-573)[[567]](#cite_note-574) The magazine named her the [world's highest-paid female musician](/wiki/Forbes_list_of_the_world%27s_highest-paid_musicians#Female "Forbes list of the world's highest-paid musicians") in 2011 with $90 million, and the fourth [top-earning female musician of the 2010s](/wiki/Forbes_list_of_highest-earning_musicians#2010s "Forbes list of highest-earning musicians") with $500 million.[[568]](#cite_note-575)[[569]](#cite_note-576) In 2012, she placed fourth on *Billboard*'s list of the top moneymakers of 2011 with earnings of $25 million, which included sales from *Born This Way* and her Monster Ball Tour.[[570]](#cite_note-billboard-577)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** [/ˈstɛfəni ˌdʒɜːrməˈnɒtə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-StefaniGermanotta.ogg "File:En-us-StefaniGermanotta.ogg") [*STEF-ən-ee JUR-mə-NOT-ə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")
2. **[^](#cite_ref-99)** In 2010, Fusari claimed he was entitled to a 20% share of the company's earnings, but the [New York Supreme Court](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court "New York Supreme Court") dismissed both the lawsuit and a counter-suit by Gaga.[[96]](#cite_note-97)[[97]](#cite_note-98)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-126)** Both of the fragrances were released in association with [Coty](/wiki/Coty "Coty").[[122]](#cite_note-pressrelease-124)[[123]](#cite_note-125)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-311)** Promoter [Live Nation](/wiki/Live_Nation_Entertainment "Live Nation Entertainment") and Gaga's representatives estimated an audience of 2.5 million,[[305]](#cite_note-308)[[306]](#cite_note-309) whilst the local authorities claimed a number of 2.1 million.[[307]](#cite_note-310)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-448)** Gaga says that the song "Poker Face" was about her bisexuality, and she openly speaks about how her past boyfriends were uncomfortable with her sexual orientation.[[32]](#cite_note-rsannual-33)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-556)** As of February 2024, Gaga has had cumulative single certifications of 82.5 million digital downloads and on-demand streaming as a solo artist, and 5 million with Bradley Cooper.[[550]](#cite_note-555)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-573)** In February 2016, *Forbes* estimated Gaga's net worth at $275 million.[[566]](#cite_note-572)
